content.text: Why Automated Changelogs Matter for GenAI Services

GenAI services have a stakeholder audience that conventional software does not: the teams that consume the service via API need to know not just what code changed but what behavioral changes to expect. A changelog that lists only 'feat: add calendar integration' is less useful than one that says 'Prompt changes: calendar tool now prefers the closest future date when year is ambiguous.' Automated changelogs derived from structured commit messages produce this level of specificity without manual authoring effort.
For internal teams, changelogs also serve as a paper trail for compliance and safety audits — a dated, versioned record of what prompt and guardrail changes shipped in each release.

attrs.alt: Changelog pipeline: git log → parser → grouper → renderer → CHANGELOG.md, GitHub Release, Slack

content.text: The Full Changelog Script
The following script reads conventional commits between two git references (tags, SHAs, or branch names) and produces a structured Markdown changelog with GenAI-specific sections.

# scripts/generate_changelog.py
import subprocess, re, sys
from collections import defaultdict, OrderedDict
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class CommitEntry:
    sha: str
    commit_type: str
    scope: str
    description: str
    is_breaking: bool
    eval_cases: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    refs: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

SECTION_ORDER = [
    ("guardrail", "Guardrail Changes"),
    ("prompt",    "Prompt Changes"),
    ("tool",      "Tool Schema Changes"),
    ("agent",     "Agent Changes"),
    ("feat",      "New Features"),
    ("fix",       "Bug Fixes"),
    ("hotfix",    "Emergency Fixes"),
    ("perf",      "Performance"),
    ("eval",      "Eval Updates"),
    ("refactor",  "Refactoring"),
]

SUBJECT_RE = re.compile(r'^(\w+)(\([^)]+\))?(!)?: (.+)$')

def parse_commit(sha: str, message: str) -> CommitEntry | None:
    lines = message.strip().splitlines()
    if not lines:
        return None
    m = SUBJECT_RE.match(lines[0])
    if not m:
        return None
    commit_type, scope_raw, bang, description = m.groups()
    scope = scope_raw[1:-1] if scope_raw else ""
    is_breaking = bool(bang) or any("BREAKING CHANGE" in l for l in lines)
    eval_cases = []
    refs = []
    for line in lines:
        if line.startswith("Eval-case:"):
            eval_cases = [c.strip() for c in line.replace("Eval-case:", "").split(",")]
        elif line.startswith("Refs:"):
            refs = [r.strip() for r in line.replace("Refs:", "").split(",")]
    return CommitEntry(sha[:7], commit_type, scope, description, is_breaking, eval_cases, refs)

def git_commits(since: str, until: str = "HEAD") -> list[CommitEntry]:
    raw = subprocess.check_output(
        ["git", "log", f"{since}..{until}", "--format=%H%n%B%n---END---", "--no-merges"],
        text=True
    )
    entries = []
    for block in raw.split("---END---"):
        block = block.strip()
        if not block:
            continue
        lines = block.splitlines()
        sha = lines[0].strip()
        if not sha or len(sha) != 40:
            continue
        message = "\n".join(lines[1:])
        entry = parse_commit(sha, message)
        if entry:
            entries.append(entry)
    return entries

def render_markdown(entries: list[CommitEntry], version: str, date: str) -> str:
    breaking = [e for e in entries if e.is_breaking]
    by_type = defaultdict(list)
    for e in entries:
        by_type[e.commit_type].append(e)

    parts = [f"## [{version}] - {date}\n"]

    if breaking:
        parts.append("### ⚠ Breaking Changes\n")
        for e in breaking:
            scope_str = f"**{e.scope}**: " if e.scope else ""
            parts.append(f"- {scope_str}{e.description} ({e.sha})")
        parts.append("")

    for type_key, section_title in SECTION_ORDER:
        commits = by_type.get(type_key, [])
        if not commits:
            continue
        parts.append(f"### {section_title}\n")
        for e in commits:
            scope_str = f"**{e.scope}**: " if e.scope else ""
            eval_str = f" [evals: {', '.join(e.eval_cases)}]" if e.eval_cases else ""
            refs_str = f" ({', '.join(e.refs)})" if e.refs else ""
            parts.append(f"- {scope_str}{e.description} ({e.sha}){eval_str}{refs_str}")
        parts.append("")

    return "\n".join(parts)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    since = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "v1.0.0"
    version = sys.argv[2] if len(sys.argv) > 2 else "NEXT"
    date = sys.argv[3] if len(sys.argv) > 3 else "2024-01-15"
    entries = git_commits(since)
    print(render_markdown(entries, version, date))
Integrating Changelog Generation into CI
The changelog generator runs as a post-merge job that updates CHANGELOG.md and creates a GitHub release on every merge to main that advances the version.

# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Create Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - 'v*.*.*'

jobs:
  create-release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0   # full history for changelog generation

      - name: Get previous tag
        id: prev
        run: |
          CURRENT="${{ github.ref_name }}"   # e.g. v1.3.0
          PREV=$(git tag --sort=-version:refname | grep -v "$CURRENT" | head -1)
          echo "tag=$PREV" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - name: Generate changelog
        id: changelog
        run: |
          DATE=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%d)
          python scripts/generate_changelog.py \
            "${{ steps.prev.outputs.tag }}" \
            "${{ github.ref_name }}" \
            "$DATE" > /tmp/release_notes.md
          cat /tmp/release_notes.md

      - name: Update CHANGELOG.md
        run: |
          # Prepend new section to existing CHANGELOG.md
          cat /tmp/release_notes.md CHANGELOG.md > /tmp/new_changelog.md
          mv /tmp/new_changelog.md CHANGELOG.md
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git add CHANGELOG.md
          git commit -m "docs(changelog): update for ${{ github.ref_name }}"
          git push origin HEAD:main

      - name: Create GitHub Release
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs')
            const notes = fs.readFileSync('/tmp/release_notes.md', 'utf8')
            await github.rest.repos.createRelease({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              tag_name: '${{ github.ref_name }}',
              name: '${{ github.ref_name }}',
              body: notes,
              draft: false,
              prerelease: false,
            })
Audience-Specific Release Notes

A single changelog serves the development team. Operators and downstream API consumers need a different view: they care about behavioral changes that affect how they use the service, not internal refactoring or eval additions.

# scripts/generate_release_notes.py
# Produces audience-specific release notes from the same commit history.

from generate_changelog import git_commits, CommitEntry

OPERATOR_SECTIONS = {
    "guardrail": "Safety and Content Changes",
    "prompt":    "Behavioral Changes",
    "tool":      "Available Tools",
    "agent":     "Service Behavior",
    "feat":      "New Capabilities",
    "hotfix":    "Critical Fixes",
}

DEVELOPER_ONLY_TYPES = {"refactor", "test", "ci", "chore", "build", "style", "eval"}

def render_operator_notes(entries: list[CommitEntry], version: str) -> str:
    relevant = [e for e in entries if e.commit_type in OPERATOR_SECTIONS]
    if not relevant:
        return f"## {version}\n\nNo operator-visible changes in this release.\n"

    from collections import defaultdict
    by_type = defaultdict(list)
    for e in relevant:
        by_type[e.commit_type].append(e)

    parts = [f"# Release {version} — What Changed\n"]
    for type_key, section_title in OPERATOR_SECTIONS.items():
        commits = by_type.get(type_key, [])
        if not commits:
            continue
        parts.append(f"## {section_title}\n")
        for e in commits:
            # Use the commit description directly — these should already be human-readable
            breaking_marker = " **[Breaking]**" if e.is_breaking else ""
            parts.append(f"- {e.description}{breaking_marker}")
        parts.append("")
    return "\n".join(parts)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    since = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "v1.0.0"
    version = sys.argv[2] if len(sys.argv) > 2 else "NEXT"
    entries = git_commits(since)
    print(render_operator_notes(entries, version))
Publishing to Slack and Other Channels

Release notes for GenAI behavioral changes should be broadcast to the teams that consume the service as soon as a release is tagged. A Slack notification with the guardrail and prompt sections of the changelog ensures that consumer teams are not surprised by behavioral shifts.
Keeping the Changelog Machine-Readable

In addition to the Markdown changelog for humans, a JSON changelog enables downstream automation: a monitoring tool can read the latest changelog to know which eval cases were added in the most recent release, or a deployment tool can verify that a hotfix commit is present in the release being deployed.
Changelog Hygiene Rules
Rule
Rationale
Commit descriptions must stand alone — no jargon or internal refs
The changelog reads the description verbatim; 'fix ticket 342' is useless to a downstream consumer
Scope must be present for prompt, tool, guardrail, and agent commits
Without scope, 'Prompt Changes' becomes a list of unattributable descriptions
BREAKING CHANGE footer must be present when output format changes
The changelog generator uses this to create the Breaking Changes section; missing it silently drops the warning
Do not squash multi-commit PRs into a single commit with a generic message
A squash commit 'feature: add calendar integration' loses all the granular type/scope information
Delete changelog entries when reverting a change
A reverted change that remains in the changelog misleads operators about what the release contains
Summary

Automated changelog generation for GenAI services turns structured commit messages into a layered communication artifact: a developer-facing Markdown changelog, an operator-facing behavioral summary, a machine-readable JSON artifact, and a real-time Slack notification. The pipeline is only as good as the commit messages that feed it — accurate, present-tense descriptions with correct type tokens and scopes produce changelogs that inform without requiring manual curation.
Changelog test:

share the generated changelog with a downstream API consumer before each release and ask if it tells them what they need to know. If they say 'yes but we still need to test everything manually,' the descriptions are too vague — improve the commit message quality, not the changelog template.
